description: Periods where CBOE VIX daily close exceeded 30 for at least 10 consecutive trading days. VIX > 30 is the standard 'elevated fear' threshold in the volatility literature (Whaley 2009 uses VIX > 30 to define his 'investor fear gauge' spikes). Our detector uses 21-day realized vol percentile rather than VIX directly, but the VIX-sourced windows provide the academically-grounded ground truth. Brief intraday spikes (Aug 2015 flash, Feb 2018 Volmageddon) do not qualify — VIX closed above 30 for only 1-2 days in those cases. The Aug 2002 dot-com window is excluded: testdata starts Jul 30 2002 and the window opens Aug 5 — the 21-day realized-vol lookback has only 5 days of history, so any lag measurement is meaningless.
dateSource: CBOE VIX daily closing prices. https://www.cboe.com/tradable_products/vix/vix_historical_data/. Ticker ^VIX on Yahoo Finance.

eventWindows.label: GFC: VIX > 30 sustained ~170 trading days. VIX peaked 80.86 on Nov 20 2008 (Lehman → trough).
US debt ceiling / EU sovereign crisis: VIX > 30 sustained ~45 trading days. VIX peaked 48 on Aug 8 2011.
COVID crash: VIX > 30 sustained ~55 trading days. VIX peaked 82.69 on Mar 16 2020.

passCriterion: Detector fires within 15 trading days of window start. Coverage >= 50% of window required. Entry lag is 15d (not 10d) because the realized-vol percentile signal builds over 21 days.
